explanation: The requirement states that the atomic proposition 'prop_0' should never be true (i.e., it is always false) throughout the system execution. In LTL, this is expressed by 'Globally not prop_0'.
The requirement states that 'prop_0' must never occur during system operation. In LTL, 'Globally not prop_0' (G !prop_0) expresses that at every point in time, prop_0 is false.
The requirement specifies that 'prop_0' should never happen at any point in time. In LTL, this is expressed by saying that globally (G), 'prop_0' is not true.
The requirement specifies that 'prop_0' should never occur. In LTL, this is represented by stating that globally (always), 'prop_0' does not hold.
The requirement states that the atomic proposition 'prop_0' must never be true at any point in time. In LTL, this is represented globally by '!prop_0', meaning 'not prop_0' is always true.
The requirement specifies that 'prop_0' should never hold at any point in time. In LTL, this is represented using the globally ('G') operator with negation on the atomic proposition.
The requirement specifies that 'prop_0' should not occur at any point in time. In LTL, 'globally not prop_0' (G !prop_0) indicates that prop_0 is always false.
The requirement states that 'prop_0' must never happen. In LTL, to express that a proposition is never true at any time, we use the globally (G) operator with its negation.
The requirement states that 'prop_0' should never occur, i.e., at all times, 'prop_0' is not true. The LTL operator 'G' (globally) with negation expresses this.
The statement requires that 'prop_0' must never be true at any point in the future. In LTL, to specify that something is never true, we use the 'globally' (G) operator with negation.
